question: Did the review adhere to pre-defined objectives and eligibility criteria?
answers: nan

question: Were the eligibility crieria appropriate for the review question?
answers:
The purpose of the
current systematic review and meta-analysis is to summarize all available evidence to investigate if schizophrenia is associated with a higher risk of psoriasis.

2.2 Inclusion criteria
Studies that investigated the risk of psoriasis among patients with schizophrenia are included. They could be either cohort or case-control studies. For cohort studies, they  needed to have a cohort of patients with schizophrenia and a cohort of comparators without schizophrenia. They needed to report relative risk or hazard ratio with 95% confidence interval (CI) of incident psoriasis after the index date. For case-control studies,
they needed to have patients with psoriasis as cases and subjects without psoriasis as controls. They needed to report odds ratio with 95% CI, comparing the odds of schizophrenia between cases and controls.

question: Were any restrictions in eligibility criteria based on sources of info appropriate (e.g. publication status or format, language, availability of data)?
answers: No language limitation was applied.

question: Did the search include an appropriate range of databases/ electronic sources for published and unpublished reports?
answers:
2.1 Search strategy
Two investigators (P.U. and K.W.) independently searched published studies indexed in MEDLINE and EMBASE database from inception to May 2017 using the search strategy described in supplementary data. No language limitation was applied. A manual search of the bibliographies of selected original and review articles was also performed.

question: Were efforts made to minimise error in selection of studies?
answers: Study eligibility was independently determined by the two in- vestigators (P.U. and W.K.). The third investigator (W.C.) reviewed all the included studied to ensure that the inclusion criteria were met and also served as the deciding vote when the first 2 investigators had different determinations of each study's eligibility

question: Were efforts made to minimise error in data collection?
answers:
2.3 Data extraction
A standardized data collection form was used to extract the following information: first author's name, title of the study, year of publication, year of study, country where the
study was conducted, number of subjects, study population, diagnosis of schizophrenia, diagnosis of psoriasis, confounders that were adjusted and adjusted effect estimates with
95% CI. This data extraction was independently performed by the 2 investigators (P.U. and K.W.) to ensure the accuracy. Any discrepancies found in the case record forms were
resolved by referring back to the original articles.

answers: Table 1: Main characteristics of the studies included in this meta-analysis

question: Were all relevant study results collected for use in the synthesis?
answers: Table 1: Main characteristics of the studies included in this meta-analysis

question: Was risk of bias (or methodological quality) formally assessed using appropriate criteria?
answers: Quality of the in-cluded studies was appraised using the Newcastle-Ottawa quality assessment scale. Using this scale, each study was assessed in three main  areas including (1) the selection of the study subjects (2) the comparability between the groups and (3) the ascertainment of the outcomes of interest (cohort studies) or the exposures of interest (case-control studies) (Stang, 2010).

question: Were efforts made to minimise error in risk of bias assessment?
answers: nan

question: Did the synthesis include all studies that it should?
answers:
The search strategy yielded 294 potentially relevant articles (245 articles from EMBASE and 49 articles from Medline). After excluding 47 duplicated articles, 247 articles underwent title and abstract review.
227 articles were excluded as they were review articles, editorial, case reports, correspondence, animal studies or interventional studies, leaving 20 articles for full-length article review. Five articles were ex-cluded at this stage as they reported risk of schizophrenia among pa-tients with psoriasis (not risk of psoriasis among patients with schizo-phrenia) whereas 9 articles were excluded because they did not report the outcome of interest. Therefore, 2 retrospective cohort studies and 4 case-control studies with approximately 6.1 million participants met
the pre-defined eligibility criteria and were included in the meta-ana-lysis  (Benros et al., 2014; Han et al., 2011; Kumar
et al., 2013; Schmitt and Ford, 2010; Tu et al., 2017; Yu et al., 2017). Figure 1 outlines the
literature review and selection process. The main characteristics and Newcastle-Ottawa
scores of the included studies are described in table 1

Table 1: Main characteristics of the studies included in this meta-analysis

Figure 2: Forest plot of all included studies

question: Were all pre-defined analyses reported or departures explained?
answers:
2.4 Statistical analysis
Review Manager 5.3 software from the Cochrane Collaboration (London, United Kingdom) was used for the statistical analyses. Adjusted point estimates and standard errors from each study were pooled together using the generic inverse variance method of DerSimonian and Laird (DerSimonian and Laird, 1986). Given the high likelihood of between study variance due to different populations and study design, random-effect model, rather than fixed-effect model, was utilized. OR from case-control studies was used as an estimate to pool the effect estimates with relative risk from cohort study. Cochran's Q test was used to evaluate the statistical heterogeneity. The test was complemented with the I2 statistic,
which quantifies the proportion of total variation across studies that is due to heterogeneity rather than chance. A value of I2 of 0% to 25% indicates insignificant heterogeneity, 26% to 50% indicates low heterogeneity, 51% to 75% indicates moderate heterogeneity and indicates 76% to 100% high heterogeneity (Higgins et al., 2003). Funnel plot and Egger's regression test were used to assess the presence of publication bias. Egger's regression test was performed using Comprehensive Meta-Analysis Software version 2.2 (Englewood, NewJersey, United States).

The pooled relative risk of psoriasis among patients with schizophrenia versus nonschizophrenia subjects was 1.83 (95% CI, 1.36-2.47). The statistical heterogeneity of this meta-analysis was moderate with an I2 of 73%. Subgroup analysis by study design revealed pooled relative risk of 1.48 (95% CI, 0.82 - 2.64; I2 68%) for case-control studies and pooled relative risk of 2.22 (95% CI, 1.95 - 2.52; I2 0%) for cohort studies. Figure 2 demonstrates the forest plot of this meta-analysis.
Sensitivity analyses were conducted by excluding the study by Tu et al. and Yu et al.
from the overall analysis (one study at a time) as both studies used the Taiwan National
Health Institute Database and may have included the same patients. The results of the
sensitivity analyses were not significantly different from the overall analysis. The pooled
relative risk was 1.71 (95% CI 1.20 - 2.43) after exclusion of the study by Tu et al. and the
pooled relative risk was 1.65 (95% CI 1.08 - 2.51) after exclusion of the study by Yu et al.

3.1 Evaluation for publication bias
Visualization of funnel plot (figure 3) did not provide suggestive evidence of
publication bias in favor of positive studies as the graph was relatively symmetric. In
addition, there was no evidence of publication bias based on Egger's regression test (p =
0.49).

question: Was between-study variation (heterogeneity) minimal or addressed in the synthesis?
answers:
Cochran's Q test was used
to evaluate the statistical heterogeneity. The test was complemented with the I2 statistic, which quantifies the proportion of total variation across studies that is due to heterogeneity rather than chance. A value of I2 of 0% to 25% indicates insignificant heterogeneity, 26% to 50% indicates low heterogeneity, 51% to 75% indicates moderate heterogeneity and indicates 76% to 100% high heterogeneity (Higgins et al., 2003). 

The pooled relative risk of psoriasis among patients with schizophrenia versus nonschizophrenia subjects was 1.83 (95% CI, 1.36-2.47). The statistical heterogeneity of this meta-analysis was moderate with an I2 of 73%. Subgroup analysis by study design revealed pooled relative risk of 1.48 (95% CI, 0.82 - 2.64; I2 68%) for case-control studies and pooled relative risk of 2.22 (95% CI, 1.95 - 2.52; I2 0%) for cohort studies. Figure 2 demonstrates the forest plot of this meta-analysis. 

Another limitation is the statistical heterogeneity that was moderate-to-high in this analysis. Difference in study design could be responsible to this as subgroup analysis per study design revealed lower I2 for both subgroups (compared with the overall analysis). It should be noted that the I2 of cohort study subgroup was 0% which may reflect the similarity between
the 2 studies that were both retrospective cohort studies using national databases. On the other hand, the I2
of case-control study subgroup remained relatively high which may reflect the nature of case-control study that is more prone to bias and, thus, higher likelihood of scattered results

question: Were biases in primary studies minimal or addressed in the synthesis?
answers:
Table 1: Main characteristics of the studies included in this meta-analysis

The major strength of this study was that of the nature of systematic review and meta-analysis that  comprehensively combined all evidence together. The primary studies included in this analysis were also of high quality as reflected by the high Newcastle-Ottawa Scores. Nonetheless, the study had some limitations and the interpretation of the results should be done with caution. The major limitation is that all of the included studies (except for the study by Kumar et al.) were conducted using electronic medical record database and relied on diagnostic codes to identify schizophrenia and psoriasis. Diagnostic codes in
electronic medical record database are known to have low sensitivity and specificity which might jeopardize the validity of the effect estimates from the primary studies. Another limitation is the statistical heterogeneity that was moderate-to-high in this analysis. Difference in study design could be responsible to this as subgroup analysis per study design revealed lower I2 for both subgroups (compared with the overall analysis). It should be noted that the I2 of cohort study subgroup was 0% which may reflect the similarity between the 2 studies that were both retrospective cohort studies using national databases. On the
other hand, the I2 of case-control study subgroup remained relatively high which may reflect the nature of case-control study that is more prone to bias and, thus, higher likelihood of scattered results.
